(31) While performing the work, monitor the condition of the item of plant or equipment throughout the work to ensure unforeseen hazards or changing conditions do not cause injury.

(32) On completion of the work:

1. return the item of plant or equipment to a safe and serviceable condition;
2. ensure each worker removes their own safety LOTO. Only the worker who was issued with a given LOTO can remove them. Contact the CFM or other person in control if for any reason it is not possible to comply with this rule;
3. ensure the person who fitted the out of service tag removes it;
4. test the equipment for correct operation; and
5. report to CFM and return all the borrowed items.

### Incomplete Work

(33) If a person who is either working alone or in a work group leaves the work area and the equipment is still out of service for any reason, they shall:

1. conduct necessary tests and/or inspections to ensure the site shall remain safe in their absence;
2. advise remaining workers that they are vacating the work area and their intention to remove their personal danger and isolation lock;
3. replace their personal isolation and danger tags with equipment isolation locks and caution/out of service tags or confirm with other workers and the CFM to take control of the isolation process;
4. test for or confirm positive isolation; and
5. arrange the management of the plant and equipment isolation lock key with the LOTO person in control or CFM. This may be at the asset location or a central point. Details of key location are to be recorded on the associated caution/out of service tags.

# Section 5 - Locking out all Isolation Points

### Lockout Devices

(34) A wide range of devices are available for locking out energy sources and other hazards. As well as interlocked guards, lockout devices can include padlocks and chains, safety lockout hasps which use multiple padlocks, switches with a built-in lock, lockouts for circuit breakers, fuses, and valves. Only devices that incorporate a lock or can accommodate one or more padlocks are suitable for locking out energy sources.

### One Person, One Lock

(35) If more than one person is working on the same plant, each person should attach their own lock to prevent the energy isolator being opened before all locks have been removed or opened. The isolation procedure should identify common lockout points to make sure energy cannot be restored while someone is still working on the plant. If two or more people are working on plant that is isolated through several lockout points, each person should attach a lock and tag to each lockout point.

(36) The need for multiple locks on each lockout point can be avoided by using a lock box. The lock box system uses only one lock at each lockout point. Keys to the locks of the plant's lockout points are inside a box which is locked by all the individual locks of people working on the same plant.

### One Lock, One key

(37) Each person working on the plant should have their own lock, key and tag. There should be no duplicate key available for any lock, except a master or duplicate key for use in an emergency. The master or duplicate key should be secured with CFM and not readily available except in an emergency.

(38) During plant inspection, repair, maintenance, cleaning or adjustment, each authorised competent worker should only hold the one key to their lock. That person is responsible for both locking and unlocking the lockout device. If there are multiple energy sources or hazard must be locked out to enable safe shutdown of the plant, the same person should hold the single key to each lockout device.

# Section 6 - Tagging

(39) A tag is not an effective isolation device. A tag acts only as a means of providing information to others at the workplace. A lock should be used as an isolation device. However, where a lock is used a tag should also be incorporated to explain the purpose of the lockout. Commonly used warning tags are outlined below.

### Personal Danger Tags

(40) A personal danger tag on the isolation devices of an item of plant or equipment is a warning that it is unsafe and that operating it may endanger the authorised competent worker who attached the tag. Personal danger tags should be restricted to workers who are authorised and competent to work on the plant or equipment. A personal danger tag should be attached to an isolator in a visible position whenever the isolator is used to lock out an energy source to allow work to be done. The personal danger tag should accompany each lock used in an isolation procedure and should identify the person who put the tag and lock in place, the time and date this occurred and the item of plant being isolated.

(41) The authorised competent worker who is working on the task should fasten their personal danger tag on all lockout devices involved in the isolation procedure. If more than one person is involved in the work, each person should attach their own lock and personal danger tag to the lockout device. Locks are available that have a personal danger tag incorporated to make sure the tag cannot be removed by any person other than the person who attached it. Removal of a personal danger tag from an isolating device should be carried out as soon as possible after completing the work. Only the person whose name is written on a personal danger tag should remove the tag. This should be done before leaving the worksite at the end of the shift.

(42) Where work on plant is not completed by the end of a working shift and the plant is required to remain isolated, arrangements should be made for out of service tags to be placed on each isolating point before the removal of personal danger tags. If work on the plant or equipment is to continue during the next shift, there should be a 'hand over' briefing by the shift leaving the site to the shift taking over the work. The briefing should include the status of the work and the removal or replacement of personal danger tags and locks.

### Out of Service Tags

(43) An out of service tag is a notice that states appliances or equipment are out of operation for repairs and alteration, or plant that is still being installed. Do not operate an appliance or equipment with an out of service tag attached. Out of service tags should not be relied upon to provide personal protection. An out of service tag on an item of plant or equipment shows that the plant is unserviceable and should not be used. An out of service tag can be attached to non-powered plant such as ladders, jacks and trolleys and powered plant. Out of service tags should be attached to the main controls if possible, or to a prominent part if there are no controls, such as with damaged ladder.

(44) An authorised competent person with specific knowledge relating to the plant should attach out of service tags. Out of service tags should be placed on devices which isolate energy sources only when those devices are set in the 'off' or 'safe' position. Before attaching an out of service tag, all required details on the tag should be clearly entered in the spaces provided, with emphasis given to the reason for placing the tag. Tags should be securely fixed so they are clearly visible.

(45) In the absence of any personal danger tag or lock, removal of an out of service tag releases plant for use, and should not be done before ensuring that:

1. All people known to have been working on the plant are clear of the plant. An inspection of the plant shows that all machinery guards are in place, that all protective devices are functional, that all maintenance tools and aids have been removed, and that the equipment is safe for normal use. Out of service tags should provide a clear 'Do Not Operate' warning and warn that failure to obey may cause damage to the equipment and/or injury to a person. It is essential that isolating mechanisms which have out of service tags attached are not switched, manipulated, or interfered with while the tags are in place.

(46) Personal danger tags and out of service tags should not be used together on the same item of equipment because they relate to different circumstances. An out of service tag should be removed when a personal danger tag is added, and vice versa. On completion of maintenance, cleaning, or other work, remove the tags before the plant returns to operation. Only authorised competent persons responsible for installing the tags should remove them.

# Section 7 - Testing Isolation Procedure

(47) After plant has been shut down, locked out and tagged, all isolated power sources should be tested, first with appropriate instruments and then by trying to re-activate the plant, before any authorised competent person starts work. A person who understands the complexity of the plant, or parts of the plant should do testing and starting the plant, including remote control stations and computers.

(48) It is not safe to assume an isolator has locked out an electricity source just because the isolator is in an open position. While the open position should create an air gap between contact points, electricity can weld the points together. If the contact points become welded together, the points remain connected even when the isolator appears to be open. Work on the plant should not begin until tests have confirmed it is safe to do so. Before using any instruments that test isolation procedure, ensure the testing equipment is calibrated and working properly.

(49) An authorised competent person involved in the task should ensure the isolation is completed and correct before commencing work. All workers approaching and accessing LV and HV electrical equipment for either operational or maintenance activities (including isolation and inspection) should ensure adequate steps for personal protection from possible electrical arc flash and touch voltages are considered.

# Section 8 - Training and Competency

(50) The employer shall provide training to ensure that the purpose and function of the energy control program are understood by employees and that the knowledge and skills required for the safe application, usage, and removal of the energy controls are acquired by employees.

### Each "Authorized Employee"

(51) Shall receive training in the recognition of applicable hazardous energy sources. The type and magnitude of the energy available in the workplace. The methods and means necessary for energy isolation and control.

### Each "Affected Employee"

(52) Shall be instructed in the purpose and use of the energy control program. All "other employees" whose work operations are or may be in an area where energy control procedures may be utilized, shall be instructed about the procedure, and about the prohibition relating to attempt to restart or reenergize machines or equipment which are locked or tagged out.

### Retraining

(53) Shall be provided for all authorized employees and affected employees whenever: There is a change in their job assignments. A change in machines, equipment or processes that present a new hazard. When there is a change in the employer's LOTO program.

### Additional Training

(54) Whenever a periodic inspection reveals noncompliance. Whenever the employer has reason to believe that there are deviations from or inadequacies in the employee’s knowledge or use of the energy control procedures. The employer shall certify that employee training has been accomplished and is being kept up to date. When a Tag Out system is used, the employees shall also be trained in the limitations of tags.

# Section 9 - Appendix

| Term                              | Definition                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
|:----------------------------------|:--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Job Hazard Analysis (JHA)         | Also called a Job Safety Analysis (JSA). This is a technique to identify the hazards and risks of specific tasks to reduce the risk of injury to workers.                                                           |
| Safe work method statement (SWMS) | In relation to high-risk construction work, is a safe work method statement mentioned in section 299 of [Work Health and Safety Regulation 2011](https://policies.uq.edu.au/directory-summary.php?legislation=266). |

question: Dr. Sarah Mitchell, a UQ academic staff member, has been approached by TechCorp Industries to provide consultancy services developing AI algorithms. However, Dr. Mitchell also holds shares in TechCorp's main competitor, DataSoft Ltd, and her spouse is employed as a senior manager at DataSoft. Additionally, the consultancy work would directly compete with a research project she is currently leading at UQ that is funded by DataSoft. Given that this consultancy must provide demonstrable benefit to UQ as specified in the Consultancy and Secondary Employment Policy, and considering the multiple conflicts of interest that must be managed according to the six management strategies outlined in the Conflict of Interest Policy, analyze: (1) which specific approval level is required for this consultancy, (2) what demonstrable benefits to UQ could justify approval despite the conflicts, and (3) which combination of the six conflict management strategies (Register, Restrict, Recruit, Remove, Relinquish, Resign) would be most appropriate to address each identified conflict while still allowing the consultancy to proceed.
gold_answer:
This complex scenario requires careful analysis of both policies to address the multiple interwoven conflicts and approval requirements.

**Approval Requirements (Consultancy and Secondary Employment Policy):**
According to Section 2, paragraph (13), consultancy must be approved by the "HR Authorisation Level 5 Authorised Officer of the Organisational Unit or above" prior to commencement. However, given the complexity of conflicts involved, this would likely need to escalate to higher levels for proper risk assessment.

**Demonstrable Benefits to UQ (Required by Policy):**
Paragraph (12) of the Consultancy and Secondary Employment Policy specifies that consultancy activities must provide benefits including: (a) generating strategic industry partnerships and expanding research opportunities, (b) maintaining industry relevance and expertise for academic dissemination, (c) diversifying income streams, and (d) developing professional capability. In this case, the AI algorithm consultancy could provide demonstrable benefit through enhanced industry partnerships with TechCorp, diversified income streams, and professional development in cutting-edge AI technologies that could benefit UQ's research and teaching programs.

**Conflict Management Strategy Analysis (Conflict of Interest Policy):**

1. **Financial Interest Conflict (TechCorp shares in competitor DataSoft):** This requires the "Relinquish" strategy from Section 2, paragraph (15). Dr. Mitchell should divest her DataSoft shares before commencing the consultancy to eliminate the financial conflict of interest.

2. **Personal Relationship Conflict (Spouse at DataSoft):** This cannot be resolved through relinquishment, so the "Restrict" strategy is most appropriate. Dr. Mitchell's involvement should be restricted from any aspects of the consultancy that could provide competitive intelligence about DataSoft, and information barriers should be established.

3. **Competing Research Project Conflict (UQ project funded by DataSoft):** This requires the "Remove" strategy. Dr. Mitchell must remove herself from the DataSoft-funded research project leadership and hand it over to another academic to avoid the direct conflict between her consultancy client (TechCorp) and her research funder (DataSoft).

4. **Overall Management:** The "Register" strategy applies to all conflicts - they must be formally disclosed in the University's conflict of interest register as required by Section 5, paragraph (27) of the Conflict of Interest Policy.

Additionally, a "Recruit" strategy should be employed by bringing in a disinterested third party (such as the Head of School or an ethics committee member) to oversee the consultancy arrangement and ensure ongoing compliance with the management plan.

The combination of Relinquish (shares), Restrict (information access), Remove (from competing research), Register (all conflicts), and Recruit (oversight) would allow the consultancy to proceed while satisfying both policies' requirements for conflict management and demonstrable University benefit.
gold_titles: Consultancy, Secondary Employment and Internal Work Policy

question: Dr. Sarah Chen is a UQ researcher who has developed a proprietary technology through her research and holds a 15% equity stake in a spin-off company, TechnoSolutions Pty Ltd, which was established through UniQuest. She has been approached by a major mining corporation to provide consultancy services (Type 2 Standard Consultancy) that would directly utilize her proprietary technology and potentially generate intellectual property that could benefit her spin-off company. The consultancy is valued at $800,000 over 18 months and would involve her as the Chief Investigator, with two research assistants and significant laboratory equipment usage. Given that this consultancy has direct connections to commercialisation activities she's involved in, analyze: (1) what specific costing, pricing, and contract management procedures must be followed according to university policy, (2) what conflict of interest disclosure and management requirements apply, and (3) how the indirect cost recovery distribution would be affected by the commercialisation connection.
gold_answer:
This scenario requires careful application of both the Research Costing and Pricing Procedure and the Conflict of Interest Policy due to the intersection of consultancy pricing and financial conflicts of interest.

**1. Costing, Pricing, and Contract Management Procedures:**

According to the Research Costing and Pricing Procedure, since this consultancy has "a direct connection to a commercialisation activity being carried out by the University commercialisation companies" (UniQuest through the spin-off), it "must be managed by these University commercialisation companies" (clause 51). This means UniQuest, not the University directly, must manage the contract.

For pricing, Dr. Chen must use the UQ Costing and Pricing Tool (clause 5) to calculate all direct costs including: her full academic salary as Chief Investigator (which "must be fully priced for consultancy projects" per clause 16), the two research assistants' salaries with on-costs, and laboratory equipment usage on a fee-for-service basis (clause 30). The 50% minimum indirect cost recovery rate applies (clause 33), and UniQuest has discretion to apply an additional Commercialisation Management Fee of up to 17.5% (clause 52).

As this is a Type 2 Standard Consultancy with commercial entities, variations to the indirect cost recovery rate cannot be sought (clause 41), and the project should be priced at market rates reflecting the value and uniqueness of her expertise (clause 44).

**2. Conflict of Interest Disclosure and Management:**

Under the Conflict of Interest Policy, Dr. Chen faces a "financial conflict of interest" where her "primary interest of maintaining and promoting the University's vision, mission and values is put at risk through the potential conflict of a significant financial interest" (clause 13) - namely, her 15% equity stake in TechnoSolutions.

She must disclose this conflict through the University's disclosure tool and work with her supervisor to develop a conflict of interest management plan (clause 18). The conflict involves both "private interests" (clause 9) through her business interest in the spin-off company and potential "commercialisation or financial conflicts of interest" (clause 13).

Additionally, under the Responsible Research Management Framework Policy referenced in clause 12, she must "disclose and manage actual, potential, and perceived conflicts of interest which may relate to the design, conduct or reporting of research."

Management strategies from clause 15 could include: Register (disclose the details), Restrict (limit her involvement in certain decisions), or Recruit (have disinterested parties oversee aspects of the project).

**3. Indirect Cost Recovery Distribution:**

Because this project is managed by UniQuest as the contracting party (due to the commercialisation connection), the indirect cost distribution follows clause 54 rather than the standard University distribution in clause 50:

- 20%: Primary faculty/institute
- 20%: Secondary faculty/institute/school/centre/research group allocation  
- 60%: University commercialisation company (UniQuest)

This differs significantly from University-managed projects where the Central University receives the 60% portion. Additionally, UniQuest retains any Commercialisation Management Fee (up to 17.5%) in full.

The intersection of these policies ensures that both the financial integrity of the consultancy pricing and the management of Dr. Chen's competing interests are properly addressed through the appropriate University channels.
gold_titles: Research and Consultancy Costing and Pricing Procedure

question: Dr. Sarah Chen, an academic staff member at UQ's School of Engineering, has been approached by three different opportunities: (1) TechCorp wants to engage her for a $150,000 project involving product testing using UQ laboratories and her specialized expertise in materials science, (2) she has been invited to serve as a paid board director for GreenTech Industries (a publicly listed company that licenses UQ intellectual property she helped develop), and (3) the School of Medicine wants her to provide internal materials testing services worth $80,000 using her specialized equipment. Assuming Dr. Chen wants to pursue all three opportunities and TechCorp's project would generate a 20% mark-up above the minimum indirect cost recovery rate, analyze the approval requirements, classification of each activity, pricing methodology for the commercial work, and explain how the surplus from the TechCorp project would be distributed according to UQ policies.
gold_answer:
This scenario requires analysis across all three policy documents to properly classify each activity, determine approval requirements, and establish financial treatment.

**Activity Classification and Approval Requirements:**

1. **TechCorp Project**: This constitutes Standard Consultancy Type 2 under the Consultancy Procedure, as it involves providing UQ staff expertise and access to University laboratories/facilities to an external commercial party for product testing. The Consultancy Policy requires approval from an HR Authorisation Level 4 Authorised Officer (or above) prior to contract signature and commencement, and the work must utilize University resources and name.

2. **GreenTech Board Directorship**: This falls under Secondary Employment requiring special approval under the Consultancy Policy. Since it involves: (a) a directorship of a publicly listed company, and (b) an entity that utilizes UQ intellectual property that Dr. Chen contributed to, she must obtain prior approval from the Provost (for Academic Staff) on recommendation from the relevant HR Authorisation Level 3 Authorised Officer before commencing this role.

3. **Internal Materials Testing**: This constitutes Internal Work under the Consultancy Policy, defined as provision of work to a UQ organisational unit other than the unit to which the staff member reports, where no external client exists. This requires approval from the relevant HR Authorisation Level 5 Authorised Officer or above prior to undertaking the work.

**Pricing Methodology for TechCorp Project:**

According to the Research and Consultancy Costing and Pricing Procedure, the $150,000 TechCorp project must be priced using the UQ Costing and Pricing Tool and include:

- **Direct Costs**: Dr. Chen's academic salary (including on-costs), equipment usage fees, consumables, and any other project-specific costs
- **Indirect Costs**: Minimum 50% of total direct costs (equivalent to 1.5x multiplier) covering University overheads like insurance, space, IT, utilities, and administration
- **Mark-up**: Additional 20% above the minimum indirect cost recovery rate to reflect market pricing for commercial contracts

The Consultancy Procedure specifies that for Type 2 Standard Consultancy, the Chief Investigator salary must be fully priced into the contract (cannot be treated as in-kind).

**Surplus Distribution from TechCorp Project:**

The Research and Consultancy Costing and Pricing Procedure defines surplus as "funds left over after all direct and indirect costs have been recovered," including mark-up and academic salary. For this Type 2 consultancy where indirect costs are applied and Dr. Chen's academic salary is priced into the contract, the surplus from her salary funds can be directed to her Academic Consultancy Award (ACA) account, subject to Level 4 Authorised Officer discretion.

The indirect costs recovered ($75,000 minimum at 50% rate) would be distributed according to the standard University allocation: 20% to primary faculty (Engineering), 20% to secondary allocation based on local priorities, and 60% to Central University.

**Additional Considerations:**

All three activities must comply with the Conflict of Interest Policy referenced across all documents, particularly given the GreenTech directorship involves UQ intellectual property. The Internal Work with the School of Medicine should be conducted within existing workload where possible, and staff cannot derive personal income from internal work. The Secondary Employment Register must be updated annually to reflect the board directorship.
gold_titles: Consultancy, Secondary Employment and Internal Work Policy
Research and Consultancy Costing and Pricing Procedure

question: Dr. Sarah Chen, a UQ academic staff member, has been offered a paid directorship position with BioTech Solutions Ltd., a company that licenses and commercialises intellectual property she co-developed while at UQ. Simultaneously, BioTech Solutions has approached UQ requesting Dr. Chen to provide expert consulting services on a new product development project worth $75,000, which would build upon her original UQ research. Analyze the approval requirements, procedural pathways, and conflict management obligations that must be addressed before Dr. Chen can accept either or both opportunities, referencing the specific authorization levels, commercialisation company involvement, and disclosure requirements that apply to this multi-layered scenario.
gold_answer:
This scenario triggers complex requirements across all three policy documents due to the intersection of secondary employment, consultancy, and commercialisation activities involving UQ intellectual property.

**Secondary Employment Approval Requirements:**
Under the Consultancy Policy clause 25.2, Dr. Chen's directorship with BioTech Solutions requires prior approval because it involves "Paid employment or Directorships of another entity that utilises UQ intellectual property that the staff member has contributed to." Clause 26 specifies this approval must come from the Provost (for Academic Staff) on recommendation from the relevant HR Authorisation Level 3 Authorised Officer, as this falls under the exceptional categories requiring pre-approval.

**Consultancy Management and Commercialisation Company Role:**
The consulting engagement must be managed through UQ's commercialisation infrastructure. According to the Consultancy Policy clause 20, "Consultancies that have a direct and tangible connection to an ongoing commercialisation activity must be contracted and managed by UniQuest." The Consultancy Procedure clause 45 reinforces this, stating that the University commercialisation company "administers Standard Consultancy contracts where the contractor is commercialising, or intends to commercialise UQ IP" and ensures the consultancy "remains consistent with any IP rights granted, or to be granted, through commercialisation."

**Consultancy Classification and Approval:**
The $75,000 consulting project would be classified as Standard Consultancy Type 2 under the Consultancy Procedure clause 14, as it involves strategic work with a company commercialising UQ intellectual property. This requires approval from a Level 4 Authorised Officer under clause 5, with the commercialisation company providing "advice and support in the processing, approval, pricing and project management."

**Conflict of Interest Management:**
The Conflict of Interest Policy clause 13 specifically addresses this scenario, noting that "financial conflicts of interest may arise as staff commercialise their research" where "the primary interest of a University staff member...is put at risk through the potential conflict of a significant financial interest." Dr. Chen must disclose both the directorship and consulting opportunity under the Conflict of Interest for Members of Staff Procedure referenced in clause 5.1 of the Conflict of Interest Policy.

**Integrated Management Plan:**
The commercialisation company must develop a management plan addressing the dual relationship (Consultancy Procedure clause 45), while the conflict of interest framework requires disclosure and management strategies from the Conflict of Interest Policy clause 15 (register, restrict, recruit, remove, relinquish, or resign). The Consultancy Policy clause 7.3 mandates that "All actual, perceived and potential conflicts of interest must be disclosed and managed" in accordance with all three supporting procedures.

**Sequential Process:**
Dr. Chen must first obtain secondary employment approval from the Provost, then work with the commercialisation company to structure the consultancy appropriately, while simultaneously managing the disclosed conflicts of interest through an approved management plan that ensures her dual roles don't compromise UQ's interests or the integrity of either arrangement.
gold_titles: Consultancy, Secondary Employment and Internal Work Policy

question: Dr. Sarah Chen's research laboratory at UQ has been approved to acquire 2 kg of sodium perchlorate (70% concentration) for a new electrochemistry project. As the laboratory's Supervisor, she needs to ensure compliance with both the Chemical Manifest Procedure and the Chemicals of Security Concern Procedure. Given that her laboratory currently updates its chemical register annually and has never held CSCs before, what specific sequence of actions must Dr. Chen take regarding: (1) the approval and acquisition process, (2) the different update frequency requirements for the two systems, and (3) the distinct reporting chains for significant changes? Your answer must detail how the requirements from both procedures interact and identify any potential conflicts in the management systems.
gold_answer:
Dr. Chen must navigate a complex intersection of requirements from both procedures, as sodium perchlorate at 70% concentration is both a hazardous chemical requiring manifest documentation and a high-risk Chemical of Security Concern.

**Approval and Acquisition Process:**
From the CSC Procedure, Dr. Chen must first ensure her Organisational Unit has appointed a Controlled Substances Officer (CSO), as acquisition of CSC requires approval through "the appropriate UniFi workflow" with approval by "a UQ staff member nominated as a Controlled Substances Officer/Manager." The CSC Procedure explicitly prohibits "purchases of CSC using corporate credit cards" without direct approval from the Director, Health, Safety and Wellness. From the Chemical Manifest Procedure, she must also conduct a risk assessment in UQSafe before commencing work with the hazardous chemical, considering "the hazards and associated risks in relation to the use, storage and disposal."

**Update Frequency Requirements - Critical Conflict:**
Here lies a significant procedural conflict. The Chemical Manifest Procedure requires only "biannual update if no other procedure requires a higher frequency," but the CSC Procedure mandates "at least a biannual stocktake of CSC under their control" by Supervisors, with Organisational Units required to "conduct discrete, dedicated six monthly audits of CSC to review inventory." This means Dr. Chen must update the chemical register in Chemwatch biannually for manifest purposes, but conduct six-monthly stocktakes specifically for the CSC component.

**Distinct Reporting Chains for Significant Changes:**
The procedures establish parallel but different reporting chains. For the Chemical Manifest Procedure, significant changes flow: UQ workers → Managers/Supervisors → HSW Manager → HSW Division, where "HSW Managers must report in turn to the HSW Division as an update of the manifest and/or placarding might be necessary." For CSC management, discrepancies or incidents follow: UQ workers → Supervisor → "Head of the Organisational Unit and the CSO" → "Organisational Unit WHSC/HSW Manager and/or the Controlled Substances Officer" → HSW Division. Additionally, the CSO must "provide a formal report to the relevant Head of Organisational Unit on the outcome of the annual inventory check and report any discrepancies to the Director, HSW Division."

**System Integration Challenges:**
Both procedures use Chemwatch for inventory management, but the CSC Procedure requires that "registers are only accessed by Organisational Units' authorised personnel with CSC flagged in the manifest" and recommends using UQSafe's "Restricted" function for risk assessments "to prevent the risk assessments becoming an uncontrolled source of information regarding storage of CSCs." The Chemical Manifest Procedure focuses on first responder access, while CSC management prioritizes security and restricted access, creating a tension between transparency and security requirements.

Dr. Chen must therefore coordinate between both systems, ensuring the sodium perchlorate is properly registered in Chemwatch for manifest purposes while maintaining the heightened security protocols required for CSCs, including more frequent monitoring and dual reporting chains to both HSW Managers and CSOs.

question: A UQ research laboratory is planning to acquire 300 litres of acetone (Class 3 flammable liquid) and 150 litres of concentrated hydrochloric acid (Class 8 corrosive) for a new project. Currently, they have 250 litres of various Class 3 flammable liquids already stored in their building. Analyze the complete compliance pathway this acquisition triggers across UQ's chemical management system, specifically detailing: (1) the risk assessment and register update requirements before acquisition, (2) the manifest reporting obligations and frequency requirements post-acquisition, and (3) the placarding implications for both the storage area and building entrance, including the specific roles of HSW Managers in coordinating these interconnected compliance requirements.
gold_answer:
This acquisition triggers a comprehensive compliance pathway across three interconnected UQ procedures:

**1. Risk Assessment and Register Update Requirements (Pre-Acquisition):**

From the Health and Safety Risk Management Procedure, prior to commencing work with hazardous chemicals, a risk assessment must be undertaken in UQSafe to assess "the overall work task and proposed use, but also the hazards and associated risks in relation to the use, storage and disposal of the hazardous chemical" (Section 13). The procedure mandates that "elimination or substitution of a hazardous chemical is the preferred control measure, by using existing chemical stocks of a less hazardous chemical instead of acquiring new stock" (Section 14).

From the Chemical Manifest Procedure, before acquisition, workers must "plan ahead when working with hazardous chemicals" and "risk assess the need to procure a hazardous chemical" (Section 9). The procedure requires consulting "current chemical registers to avoid duplication, wastage, and excessive chemical holdings" (Section 12). The Organisational Unit must update their chemical register in Chemwatch, with HSW Managers facilitating access by contacting administrators at hsw@uq.edu.au (Section 16).

**2. Manifest Reporting Obligations and Frequency:**

Post-acquisition, the Chemical Manifest Procedure requires that "trained and appointed UQ workers will update the register in Chemwatch as soon as practicable when holdings change" (Section 17). The update must reflect "hazard or dangerous goods class for existing and new stock holdings, quantities, missing or used stocks, movements or different storage location of existing holdings" (Section 18). The minimum frequency is "biannual update if no other procedure requires a higher frequency" (Section 17).

Since UQ stores hazardous chemicals "in quantities that exceed or are likely to exceed the prescribed manifest quantities in column 5, Schedule 11," it is classified as a manifest quantity workplace (MQW), requiring notification to Workplace Health and Safety Queensland under s348 of the Regulation (Section 32). Any "significant change to the quantity of hazardous chemicals" requires reporting through the chain: workers → managers/supervisors → HSW Manager → HSW Division, as "a notification to the Regulator might also then be necessary" (Section 33).

**3. Placarding Implications:**

From the Placarding Guideline, the combined quantities (550 litres of Class 3 flammable liquids total, plus 150 litres of Class 8 corrosive) must be compared against "prescribed placarding quantities in column 3 of the Schedule 11 table" (Section 21). If these quantities exceed the prescribed thresholds, placarding is required.

For the storage area, "individual storage areas where hazardous chemicals in packages are kept, must display an information placard when the prescribed placarding quantities in Schedule 11 table are exceeded" (Section 11). The placard must display appropriate "class labels required by the Regulation grouped together" with "sides not less than 100mm" (Section 12).

For the building entrance, if workplace quantities exceed prescribed limits, "an outer warning placard, or HAZCHEM sign, is required at the entrance to the workplace" with "HAZCHEM in red lettering, not less than 100 mm high, on a white or silver background" (Section 8).

**HSW Manager Coordination Role:**

HSW Managers serve as the critical coordination point across all three procedures. They "facilitate access to the relevant register (folder/space) within Chemwatch" (Chemical Manifest, Section 27), "provide support and instruction regarding completion of the chemical register" (Section 27), and must "report any significant changes to the HSW Division" (Section 27). In risk management, they "drive the development and ensure the continued relevance of their local operational risk registers" (Risk Management, Section 35). For placarding, they assist workers in "complying with the requirements" and provide "specialist advice on use, handling, storage and placarding requirements" (Placarding, Section 29).

This interconnected system ensures that chemical acquisitions are properly risk-assessed, accurately recorded in manifests, and appropriately communicated to emergency responders through placarding, with HSW Managers orchestrating compliance across all three regulatory frameworks.
gold_titles: Health and Safety Risk Management Procedure
Placarding of Chemical Storage Areas Guideline

question: Dr. Sarah Chen, a UQ researcher, removed laboratory mice from Biological Resources facilities to her own laboratory for a 72-hour behavioral study without obtaining proper Animal Ethics Committee approval or facility inspection. A colleague reported this to Research Ethics and Integrity, citing concerns about animal welfare and regulatory compliance. Given that this situation involves potential breaches of multiple research conduct requirements, analyze the complete process that would unfold: (1) What specific approvals and assessments should Dr. Chen have obtained before removing the animals according to UQ requirements? (2) How would this complaint be categorized and processed under UQ's research misconduct procedures? (3) What roles would the Pro-Vice-Chancellor (Research) and Deputy Vice-Chancellor (Research and Innovation) play in handling this matter, and what potential outcomes could result from the investigation?
gold_answer:
This complex scenario requires analysis across UQ's research governance framework, involving specific animal ethics requirements, complaint processing procedures, and institutional roles.

**Required Approvals and Assessments (Document 3 - Animal Guidelines):**
Dr. Chen should have obtained two mandatory approvals before removing laboratory animals from BR facilities. First, she needed Animal Ethics Committee (AEC) approval through a MyResearch application specifying: the exact location for animal holding, duration (her 72-hour study), scientific justification for holding animals outside BR facilities, animal care provisions, and logistics considerations including staff competencies. Second, she required a BR-facilitated inspection of her laboratory space to assess animal welfare, biosecurity, Physical Containment level certification, personnel access control, communication protocols with lab occupants, and compliance with the UQ Health Laboratory Animal Allergy guidelines. The 72-hour duration significantly exceeds the preferred <24 hours mentioned in the guidelines, requiring stronger scientific justification.

**Complaint Categorization and Processing (Document 1 - Complaints Procedure):**
This complaint would be processed under the Managing Complaints about the Conduct of Research Procedure as it involves potential breaches of both the Australian Code for the Responsible Conduct of Research and the Responsible Research Management Framework Policy. Research Ethics and Integrity would conduct a preliminary assessment to determine if the complaint, if proven, would constitute a breach. Given the clear violation of mandatory approval requirements, this would likely progress beyond preliminary assessment. The matter involves compliance failures rather than data fabrication, but the intentional circumvention of required approvals could potentially be classified as research misconduct if deemed "intentional or reckless or negligent" in failing to meet Code principles, particularly "respect for research participants, the wider community, animals and the environment."

**Institutional Roles and Potential Outcomes (Documents 1 & 2):**
The Pro-Vice-Chancellor (Research), serving as Designated Officer, would oversee the preliminary assessment and determine whether to refer the matter for investigation. If referred, they would set investigation terms of reference, appoint an investigation panel, and ensure Dr. Chen receives written notification of the complaint and investigation process. The Deputy Vice-Chancellor (Research and Innovation), as Responsible Executive Officer, would receive the final investigation report and recommendations, having ultimate authority to determine outcomes. Potential outcomes range from corrective instructions and mandatory training to more serious consequences. Given this involves regulatory compliance violations affecting animal welfare, the Deputy Vice-Chancellor could: issue corrective instructions requiring proper approvals for future work, refer the matter for misconduct proceedings under the UQ Enterprise Agreement if deemed serious enough, require additional animal ethics training, or impose research restrictions. The matter might also require disclosure to external bodies if funding agreements mandate reporting of compliance violations, as specified in the complaints procedure's confidentiality provisions.
gold_titles: Managing Complaints about the Conduct of Research Procedure
Holding Laboratory Animals Outside Biological Resources Facilities Guideline

question: An employee suffers a serious back injury in a car accident while traveling from their home to a work conference at an off-campus location. The injury requires immediate hospital treatment and will likely prevent them from performing their original duties for several months. Based on UQ's policies, what is the complete sequence of mandatory reporting and claim processes this employee must follow, and what are the specific return-to-work priority options that WIM will consider during their rehabilitation planning?
gold_answer:
This scenario requires the employee to follow a comprehensive dual-track process involving both incident reporting and workers' compensation claims, with specific return-to-work planning considerations.

**Mandatory Reporting Requirements:**

First, the employee must complete an incident report in UQSafe as required by the Health and Safety Incident Reporting Procedure (Section 6, clause 37). This creates an automatic workflow that includes notification to the Working Injury Management Team (WIM Team) among other parties (Section 3, clause 17).

**Workers' Compensation Claim Process:**

Since the injury occurred while traveling to work, the employee must submit specific mandatory forms to WIM as outlined in the Workers' Compensation Claim Guideline:

1. Application for Workers' Compensation Form (signed with witness)
2. Form 1 (with details of all treatment providers)
3. **Form 3 (declaring previous similar injuries)**
4. **Form 4 (mandatory specifically because the injury occurred while traveling to work)**
5. Workers' Compensation Medical Certificate from treating doctor

The employee has 20 business days for WIM to investigate and determine the claim (Section 3, clause 7 of the Guideline).

**Return-to-Work Priority Framework:**

Once the claim is accepted, WIM will coordinate rehabilitation planning using the four-priority framework established in the Workers' Compensation Policy (Section 2, clause 6):

- **Priority 1:** Return to original duties (unlikely given the serious nature preventing original work)
- **Priority 2:** Modified version of original duties (possible depending on functional capacity assessment)  
- **Priority 3:** Redeployment to new duties within their organizational unit or another unit
- **Priority 4:** If redeployment is impossible, employment termination on ill-health grounds under the Medical Conditions Affecting Performance Policy

**Integration Points:**

The UQSafe incident report serves dual purposes: meeting legal reporting obligations under the Work Health and Safety Act 2011 and triggering the automatic workflow that ensures WIM is immediately notified to begin workers' compensation processes. WIM's role as outlined in the Policy (Section 3, clauses 7-8) includes managing both regulatory compliance and coordinating rehabilitation programs while maintaining strict confidentiality of medical information.

This integrated approach ensures the employee receives immediate support through both safety reporting mechanisms and compensation pathways, while establishing a clear framework for their eventual return to productive work.
gold_titles: Lodging a Claim for Workers' Compensation Guideline
Health and Safety Incident and Hazard Reporting Procedure

question: An international student on a student visa is currently enrolled full-time (8 units per semester) in a CRICOS-registered program and has been granted specific building access to secured laboratory areas through their Head of Organisational Unit authorization. Due to academic difficulties, they need to reduce their enrollment to part-time status (4 units per semester) at the start of the next semester. Analyze the complete procedural cascade this student must follow across university policies, specifically addressing: (1) what immediate notification and compliance obligations arise under their enrolment conditions, (2) what ID card-related actions they must take and why, (3) how their building access authorization is affected, and (4) what additional considerations apply given their international student status and visa conditions.
gold_answer:
This scenario requires synthesizing requirements from all three policy documents to address the cascading effects of the enrollment change:

**1. Immediate Notification and Compliance Obligations (Enrolment Policy):**
Under clause 7 of the Enrolment Policy, all enrolled students must "comply with all policies and procedures applicable to students" and "comply with all conditions placed on their enrolment." The student must immediately notify the University of their intention to change enrollment status, as this affects their compliance with program requirements. Additionally, clause 7(e) specifically requires international students to "comply with the conditions of their visa," which becomes critical as the enrollment change may affect their student visa conditions requiring full-time study.

**2. ID Card Actions Required (Student ID Card Procedure):**
According to clause 9(c) of the Student ID Card Procedure, "The student is responsible for applying for a replacement ID card to reflect their enrolment status if they change their enrolment from full-time to part-time and vice versa." Since the student is changing from full-time (8 units, above the 6+ unit threshold defined in clause 7 definitions) to part-time (4 units), they must apply for a replacement ID card through the Request a Student ID Card form. This is mandatory because their current card reflects full-time status, which will no longer be accurate.

**3. Building Access Authorization Effects (Use and Management of UQ Land, Buildings and Facilities Policy + Student ID Card Procedure):**
The building access situation involves both documents. Under clauses 17-18 of the Student ID Card Procedure, students with Head of Organisational Unit authorization can have ID cards encoded for secured area access, but clause 19 requires immediate notification to UQ Security if the card is lost or stolen. Since the student must obtain a replacement ID card (due to status change), their current encoded access will be invalidated. The new card will require re-authorization from their Head of Organisational Unit for the secured laboratory areas. Furthermore, under clause 11 of the Use and Management of UQ Land, Buildings and Facilities Policy, the student must "produce their UQ-issued identification card on request when on or in UQ Facilities," making the updated card essential for continued legitimate access.

**4. International Student and Visa Considerations (Enrolment Policy):**
The most critical aspect involves clause 7(d) of the Enrolment Policy, which requires international students in CRICOS-registered programs to "have a valid Confirmation of Enrolment (CoE) and student visa for the duration of their studies in Australia." Reducing to part-time enrollment may breach their student visa conditions, which typically require full-time study. The student must ensure their visa conditions permit part-time study or seek appropriate visa variation before implementing the enrollment change. Failure to maintain visa compliance could result in enrollment cancellation under clause 9 of the Enrolment Policy, which permits cancellation for students who "do not meet the requirements in clause 7."

**Procedural Sequence:**
The student must: (1) verify visa conditions permit part-time study or obtain appropriate visa variation, (2) formally notify the University of the enrollment change, (3) apply for a replacement ID card reflecting part-time status, (4) coordinate with their Head of Organisational Unit to re-authorize building access on the new card, and (5) ensure continued compliance with all policy requirements throughout the transition. This demonstrates how enrollment changes trigger interconnected obligations across multiple university policy domains.
gold_titles: Use and Management of UQ Land, Buildings and Facilities Policy

question: A UQ research laboratory currently stores 800 kg of potassium nitrate (65% concentration) and 150 litres of nitric acid (40% concentration) for legitimate research purposes. During a routine inventory check, they discover an additional undocumented 300 kg of potassium nitrate that was donated by an external collaborator six months ago. Given that potassium nitrate has a prescribed placarding quantity of 250 kg in Schedule 11, analyze the comprehensive compliance requirements this laboratory must now fulfill. Your analysis must specify: (1) the immediate procedural steps required under each relevant UQ procedure, (2) the different reporting frequencies and notification requirements to various authorities established by each procedure, and (3) the specific roles and responsibilities that must be coordinated between different UQ personnel to achieve full compliance across all applicable procedures.
gold_answer:
This scenario triggers compliance requirements across three interconnected UQ procedures due to the presence of Chemicals of Security Concern (CSC) that exceed placarding thresholds with undocumented inventory discrepancies.

**Immediate Procedural Steps Required:**

Under the **Chemical Manifest Procedure**, the laboratory must immediately update their chemical register in Chemwatch to reflect the actual holdings (Section 2, clause 17-18). The undocumented 300 kg represents a "significant change" requiring notification to WHSQ since UQ is classified as a manifest quantity workplace (Section 6, clause 33). The appointed trained worker must update the register "as soon as practicable when holdings change" to ensure first responders have accurate information.

Under the **Chemicals of Security Concern Procedure**, potassium nitrate is a listed CSC requiring enhanced security measures (Appendix A). The discovery of undocumented CSC triggers incident reporting requirements (Section 3, clause 32-33). An incident report must be completed in UQSafe with the HSW Division invited to view it, and the area's security situation must be reassessed. The laboratory must also conduct an immediate inventory reconciliation as part of their biannual stocktake obligations (Section 4, clause 40).

Under the **Placarding Guideline**, the total 1,100 kg of potassium nitrate significantly exceeds the 250 kg prescribed placarding quantity in Schedule 11 (Section 3, Part A). Information placards must be installed at the main building entry and room entry points displaying appropriate class labels (clauses 11-12, 19). An outer warning HAZCHEM placard may be required at the workplace entrance depending on campus-wide holdings (clause 8).

**Reporting Frequencies and Notification Requirements:**

The **Chemical Manifest Procedure** requires biannual updates as minimum frequency (Section 3, clause 17), but the significant change necessitates immediate reporting to HSW Division for potential WHSQ notification under s348 of the WHS Regulation. The manifest must be available to Queensland Fire and Emergency Services per s96 of the Fire and Emergency Services Act 1990.

The **CSC Procedure** mandates biannual stocktakes specifically for CSCs (Section 4, clause 40), with formal reporting to the Head of Organisational Unit on annual inventory checks (clause 39). Any discrepancies must be reported to the Director, HSW Division, who determines if National Security Hotline notification is required (Section 6, clause 54).

The **Placarding Guideline** requires placards to be "amended as soon as practicable if there is a change in the type or quantity of goods held" (Section 3, clause 6), with Property and Facilities Division responsible for updating campus placarding and notifying Emergency Services (Section 4, clauses 27-28).

**Coordinated Roles and Responsibilities:**

The **Supervisor** must ensure the incident is reported to the Controlled Substances Officer and HSW Manager (CSC Procedure, clause 40), while simultaneously ensuring chemical register updates and risk assessment reviews (Chemical Manifest Procedure, clause 24). They must also advise Property and Facilities Division of placarding changes (Placarding Guideline, clause 24).

The **Controlled Substances Officer** must assist with inventory reconciliation, review security risk assessments for the increased CSC holdings, and provide formal reporting to the Head of Organisational Unit about the discrepancy (CSC Procedure, clauses 38-39).

The **HSW Manager** must facilitate Chemwatch access updates, assess compliance across all three procedures, and coordinate reporting to HSW Division for both manifest changes and security concerns (Chemical Manifest Procedure, clause 27; CSC Procedure, clause 43).

The **HSW Division** must determine WHSQ notification requirements for the manifest change, assess National Security Hotline reporting needs for the CSC discrepancy, and coordinate with UQ Security for area security reassessment (Chemical Manifest Procedure, clause 28; CSC Procedure, clause 43).

This multi-procedural compliance scenario demonstrates how UQ's chemical management system integrates safety, security, and emergency response requirements through coordinated documentation, reporting, and oversight mechanisms across different regulatory frameworks.
gold_titles: Placarding of Chemical Storage Areas Guideline

question: A UQ research laboratory is planning to acquire potassium perchlorate at 70% concentration for legitimate research purposes. Given that this chemical falls under the Chemicals of Security Concern requirements and considering UQ's enterprise health and safety risk management framework, analyze how the required security risk assessment process must align with UQ's Risk Appetite Statement and enterprise risk reporting structures. Specifically, explain how the "Restricted" marking capability in UQSafe for CSC risk assessments relates to UQ's enterprise risk management approach, what reporting pathways must be followed if the security risks fall outside UQ's "cautious" risk appetite level, and how the HSW Division's role in CSC management integrates with their responsibilities for enterprise health and safety risk consolidation and reporting to USET and SRAC.
gold_answer:
This scenario requires integration of CSC-specific security risk management with UQ's broader enterprise health and safety risk framework across multiple interconnected processes:

**CSC Risk Assessment Requirements and UQSafe Integration:**
According to the CSC procedure, potassium perchlorate at 70% concentration is a high-risk CSC requiring specific security risk assessment. This assessment must be completed in UQSafe and can be marked as "Restricted" to prevent uncontrolled information sharing about CSC storage locations. The Health and Safety Risk Management procedure establishes that UQSafe is UQ's central platform for risk assessments, ensuring consistency across both operational hazard assessments and specialized security assessments.

**Risk Appetite Statement Alignment:**
The Health and Safety Risk Management procedure specifies that UQ's Risk Appetite Statement (RAS) categorizes both "Student Safety" and "Employee Health, Safety and Wellness" as "cautious," meaning UQ takes a cautious approach to risk-taking. The CSC security assessment must evaluate whether the proposed acquisition and storage arrangements align with this cautious appetite. If the security risks associated with storing this high-concentration oxidizing agent exceed the cautious threshold, the Enterprise Risk Management Framework's Risk Matrix outlines a specific reporting and approval process.

**Enterprise vs. Operational Risk Integration:**
The CSC security risk operates at both levels defined in the Health and Safety procedure. At the operational level, the laboratory must conduct detailed security risk assessments covering storage, access control, and handling procedures. At the enterprise level, the HSW Division must consider how this CSC acquisition affects UQ's overall security risk profile and whether it should be included in the Enterprise Health and Safety Risk Register or the "watch list" for high inherent risk level items.

**HSW Division Reporting Integration:**
The HSW Division has dual responsibilities that must be coordinated. Under the CSC procedure, they provide technical guidance on CSC management and determine (with UQ Security) whether incidents require reporting to the National Security Hotline. Under the Health and Safety Risk Management procedure, they consolidate organizational unit risks into enterprise-level reporting through the "HSW Monthly Dashboard" to USET and SRAC, and the annual "Health and Safety Risk Register." The CSC security risks must be integrated into these broader reporting mechanisms.

**Multi-level Consultation and Approval Process:**
The CSC procedure requires the Controlled Substances Officer to function as the commodity approver in UniFi, while the Health and Safety procedure mandates consultation processes at both organizational and enterprise levels. If the security risks exceed the cautious risk appetite, the approval process must follow the ERMF Risk Matrix requirements, potentially requiring escalation to USET or SRAC through the established enterprise risk reporting channels, while simultaneously meeting the CSC-specific approval requirements through the UniFi workflow and CSO oversight.

This integration ensures that specialized chemical security risks are managed within UQ's comprehensive enterprise risk framework while maintaining the specific security controls required for chemicals of security concern.
gold_titles: Health and Safety Risk Management Procedure

question: An Organizational Unit at UQ conducts its annual workplace inspection and discovers that their combined chemical storage now exceeds the prescribed placarding quantities outlined in Schedule 11 for three different GHS categories. According to the integrated requirements of UQ's risk management procedures, what specific sequence of actions must the Head of the Organizational Unit take to ensure compliance with both the Chemical Storage Placarding Guidelines and the Health and Safety Risk Management Procedure, and how would this discovery potentially impact the unit's reporting obligations to both the HSW Division and the Enterprise Risk Management Framework?
gold_answer:
According to the integrated requirements of both documents, the Head of the Organizational Unit must take the following sequence of actions:

**Immediate Placarding Compliance (Chemical Storage Placarding Guidelines):**
1. **Update Chemical Manifest**: Ensure the Chemical Manifest is updated to accurately reflect current chemical holdings, as required by the Chemical Manifest Procedure referenced in the placarding guidelines.
2. **Determine Placarding Requirements**: Compare the quantities against Schedule 11 prescribed placarding quantities to identify specific placard types needed (outer warning placards, information placards for packages, or both).
3. **Install Required Placards**: Coordinate with Property and Facilities Division to install appropriate placards at entrance points and storage locations, ensuring they meet Schedule 13 specifications with proper materials, dimensions, and positioning.

**Risk Management Integration (Health and Safety Risk Management Procedure):**
4. **Update Organizational Unit Risk Register**: The discovery triggers an update to the unit's Health and Safety Risk Register, as this represents a change in risk exposure that must be recorded in UQSafe and consolidated into their organizational risk register.
5. **Risk Assessment Review**: Complete new risk assessments for the chemical storage areas now exceeding thresholds, determining the Managed Risk Level (MRL) and Target Risk Level (TRL) for these enhanced chemical storage risks.
6. **Control Measure Implementation**: Apply the Hierarchy of Controls to manage the increased risk exposure, potentially implementing additional controls to achieve the target risk level.

**Reporting Obligations:**
The discovery creates multiple reporting pathways:
- **To HSW Division**: The unit must provide updated information for inclusion in the UQ enterprise-level Health and Safety Risk Register, as HSW Division coordinates development and review of enterprise risks by analyzing organizational unit risks.
- **To Enterprise Risk Management**: If the chemical storage risk assessment results in "high" or "extreme" risk levels, this could impact UQ's Top Risks compilation by Enterprise Risk Services, particularly if it affects the risk appetite statement's "cautious" approach to employee health, safety and wellness.
- **Annual Reporting**: The enhanced chemical storage risks must be incorporated into the unit's annual HSW Safety Management Plan and included in their annual top health and safety risks report to the Director, HSW.

**Integration Point**: The chemical manifest updates required by the placarding guidelines directly inform the risk register updates required by the risk management procedure, creating a unified compliance approach where chemical inventory management drives both regulatory placarding and enterprise risk assessment processes.
gold_titles: Health and Safety Risk Management Procedure
Placarding of Chemical Storage Areas Guideline

question:
A UQ research laboratory discovers a significant discrepancy during their biannual stocktake: their sodium cyanide inventory shows 2.5 kg missing, which represents a 40% reduction from their registered holdings. The missing chemical was classified as a Chemicals of Security Concern under the National Code of Practice, and the laboratory's current Managed Risk Level (MRL) for chemical security was previously assessed as "Medium" in their UQSafe risk assessment. 

Given this scenario, analyze the complete procedural response required by integrating the requirements from UQ's Chemical Manifest, Health and Safety Risk Management, and Chemicals of Security Concern procedures. Your analysis must address: (1) the immediate reporting chain and timeline requirements, (2) the risk assessment review obligations and consultation processes that must occur, and (3) the manifest update and external regulator notification requirements, explaining how these three procedural frameworks interact in this specific situation.
gold_answer:
This scenario triggers a complex, interconnected response across all three UQ procedures due to the nature of sodium cyanide as both a hazardous chemical requiring manifest tracking and a Chemical of Security Concern with heightened security protocols.

**Immediate Reporting Chain and Timeline Requirements:**

Under the Chemicals of Security Concern Procedure, UQ workers must "immediately report any suspicious behaviour or suspected diversion of CSC material to their Supervisor and to the Organisational Unit's WHSC/HSW Manager and the Controlled Substances Officer." The 40% inventory discrepancy constitutes suspected diversion requiring immediate escalation. The reporting chain flows from the discovering worker → Supervisor → WHSC/HSW Manager and Controlled Substances Officer → HSW Division → potentially UQ Security and the National Security Hotline.

The Chemical Manifest Procedure reinforces this through its requirement that "UQ workers are responsible for reporting any significant and/or unexplained changes to chemicals holdings and registers to their manager or Supervisor," with managers then reporting to HSW Managers, who "must report in turn to the HSW Division."

**Risk Assessment Review and Consultation Requirements:**

The Health and Safety Risk Management Procedure mandates that risk assessments be reviewed when circumstances change significantly. A 40% inventory loss represents a substantial change requiring immediate risk assessment review in UQSafe. The current "Medium" MRL must be reassessed considering the security breach implications.

The CSC Procedure specifically requires that "risk assessments must specifically address the security risk of the CSC and the potential for unauthorised access and removal of these materials," and must be completed in UQSafe using the "Restricted" marking function. The consultation requirements from the Risk Management Procedure demand "constructive consultation with relevant stakeholders" including "those working with the hazards and those that are accountable and/or responsible for implementation."

**Manifest Update and Regulator Notification Requirements:**

The Chemical Manifest Procedure establishes that UQ must "notify the regulator when there is a significant change in the amount held" because UQ operates as a "manifest quantity workplace (MQW)" under s348 of the Work Health and Safety Regulation 2011. A 40% reduction in sodium cyanide holdings clearly constitutes a "significant change" requiring notification to Workplace Health and Safety Queensland (WHSQ).

The manifest must be updated in Chemwatch to reflect the inventory discrepancy, as the procedure requires updates "as soon as practicable when holdings change." Since sodium cyanide is listed in the CSC Procedure as requiring control at "any concentration," this loss has both manifest and security implications.

**Procedural Integration:**

These three procedures create an integrated response framework: the CSC Procedure triggers immediate security-focused reporting and investigation protocols; the Risk Management Procedure requires systematic risk reassessment with stakeholder consultation to determine new risk levels and controls; and the Chemical Manifest Procedure mandates formal regulator notification and database updates to maintain legal compliance. The HSW Division serves as the central coordination point where all three procedural streams converge, ultimately determining whether external regulator notification via the National Security Hotline is required, while ensuring manifest accuracy and appropriate risk management responses are implemented.
gold_titles: Health and Safety Risk Management Procedure
